title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
description: A path in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes in the sequence has an edge connecting them. A node can only appear in the sequence at most once. Note that the path does not need to pass through the root. Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any non-empty path.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 3 * 10^4]
thinking_questions: A path can start anywhere and end anywhere. At each node, what options do you have for the path that passes through it?
When you return a value from recursion, what does the parent need — the full path through you, or just the best single-side path?
Why do you need to take max(left, 0) and max(right, 0)?

hints.L1: At each node the best path through it = node.val + max(left_path, 0) + max(right_path, 0). But you can only extend one side to the parent.
hints.L2: DFS returns the best single-direction gain (node.val + max(one_child, 0)). At each node, update global max with node.val + max(left,0) + max(right,0).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: local = both sides; return value = best single side to parent
result = [float('-inf')]
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return 0
    left = max(dfs(node.left), 0)   # ignore negative paths
    right = max(dfs(node.right), 0)
    # path through this node (cannot be extended up through both sides)
    result[0] = max(result[0], node.val + left + right)
    # return best single-direction extension to parent
    return node.val + max(left, right)
dfs(root)
return result[0]
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: Why max(left, 0) and not just left? What does taking max with 0 represent?
Why can't you return node.val + left + right to the parent?
What if you needed to return the actual path, not just the sum?
solution_notes: DFS: update global max with both-sides sum; return best single-side gain to parent. max(child,0) ignores negative branches. O(n).
